name: Bifascicular Block
identifyBy: RBBB morphology: RSR' in V1, wide S in I/V6
PLUS Left axis deviation −45° to −90° (LAFB component)
The combination blocks 2 of 3 fascicles of the conduction system
One fascicle (left posterior) still conducting - trifascicular block if PR also prolonged
management: Bifascicular block without symptoms: no urgent treatment - cardiology referral
With syncope or near-syncope: urgent evaluation - may require pacemaker
Trifascicular block (bifascicular + 1° AV block): high risk - pacemaker strongly considered
In acute anterior MI: bifascicular block suggests extensive damage - high mortality risk
Intermittent 3° AV block causing syncope: permanent pacemaker
pearls: Bifascicular block = 2/3 fascicles blocked; trifascicular = all 3 (permanent complete heart block)
RBBB + LAFB is more common than RBBB + LPFB (LAFB more common than LPFB)
In anterior MI, new bifascicular block indicates major conduction system damage
Syncope in bifascicular block must be investigated urgently - may be Stokes-Adams attack (intermittent complete heart block)
Electrophysiology study (EPS) can assess HV interval - prolonged HV (>70ms) = high risk

qaNotes: Conduction abnormality pattern: QRS/axis morphology should be checked in diagnostic leads.
